title: StubHub reiterates 2026 outlook for $9.9B-$10.1B GMS and $400M-$420M adjusted EBITDA as margins expand
content:
Earnings Call Insights: StubHub Holdings, Inc. (STUB) Q1 2026

MANAGEMENT VIEW

 	* “We are off to a positive start in 2026 with solid top line growth and increased profitability,” said (Founder, CEO, & Chairman Eric Baker), adding, “GMS increased 7% to $2.2 billion, and adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to 16%.”
 	* Baker described 2026 priorities as “Growth in our core resale marketplace; increased profitability; and advancement of longer-term distribution opportunities,” and said Q1 execution supported “all 3 of these objectives,” while reiterating, “we are reiterating our full year outlook for GMS and adjusted EBITDA.”
 	* On open distribution, Baker said StubHub expanded supply efforts “beyond individual and professional sellers to enterprise scale sellers,” and argued, “We believe it is clear the market is heading in the direction of nonexclusive open distribution,” while citing that “a recent proposal to settle antitrust suit in the live events space included nonexclusive distribution.”
 	* Baker highlighted two product/distribution launches: “Distribution Manager: An AI-powered self-serve tool that allows artists, teams and venues to list and manage tickets directly through StubHub,” and “an integration with a primary ticketing company that tickets rights holders like Stanford University Athletics.”
 	* (Chief Financial Officer Constance James) framed the company’s financial approach as “Durable GMS growth; meaningful margin expansion; and continued strength in free cash flow generation,” and said Q1 results showed “progress in each of these areas.”

OUTLOOK

 	* James reiterated full-year targets: “We are reiterating our full year outlook for GMS and adjusted EBITDA,” guiding to “GMS of $9.9 billion to $10.1 billion” and “adjusted EBITDA of $400 million to $420 million.”
 	* On phasing, James said the company expects seasonality similar to last year: “We expect the first and second half contribution to full year GMS to remain broadly consistent with 2025.”
 	* On comparability, James said, “As expected, our reported GMS for the first quarter reflects the comparability impact from the all-in pricing implementation introduced in May last year,” and added, “We will fully lap that comparability period in the second quarter.”
 	* Management’s guidance did not provide EPS targets in the transcript.

FINANCIAL RESULTS

 	* James reported Q1 “Revenue increased 12% year-over-year to $446 million,” with “GMS… 7% to $2.2 billion.”
 	* James said, “Gross margin was 85%,” and “Sales and marketing expense was approximately 50% of revenue, representing a 500 basis point improvement year-over-year.”
 	* Profitability expanded as “Adjusted EBITDA was $72.1 million, a margin of 16%, which expanded over 400 basis points year-over-year,” and GAAP profitability was noted as “Net income for the first quarter was $48 million.”
 	* Cash and leverage commentary focused on deleveraging and liquidity: James said StubHub generated “approximately $298 million of free cash flow on a trailing 12-month basis,” ended with “approximately $1.5 billion of cash and cash equivalents,” and “Net leverage improved to approximately 4x trailing adjusted EBITDA… down from 4.5x at year-end 2025.”
 	* Post-quarter, James said, “we repaid $100 million of our U.S. dollar term loan,” bringing “total debt repayment over the last 12 months to more than $1 billion,” with “total outstanding debt… approximately $1.4 billion with no maturities until March 2030.”
 	* On equity and dilution, James said “$314 million of preferred equity… converted into approximately 16 million shares,” resulting in “374 million common shares outstanding,” and added, “we had approximately 399 million fully diluted shares as of March 31,” with expected dilution “in the low to mid-single-digit percentage range for the remainder of 2026.”

Q&A

 	* Eric Sheridan, Goldman Sachs: Asked how much operating leverage is still to come and whether StubHub would reinvest upside; (CFO James) answered, “you’re naturally going to get incremental operating leverage as the business grows,” and said she would expect “slightly more profitability flowing through towards the second half.”
 	* Doug Anmuth, JPMorgan: Asked about marketing efficiency drivers and open-distribution progress/pipeline; (CEO Baker) said leadership scale improves “liquidity… data,” and emphasized the focus is to “productize it and make it turnkey,” while highlighting Distribution Manager and primary integrations.
 	* Mark Mahaney, Evercore: Asked about ads and international growth; (CEO Baker) said advertising work is focused on “optimiz[ing] the auction mechanics,” ensuring ads “don’t impact conversion rates,” and reiterated the company is “in the testing phase,” while pointing to more tours going global as an international driver.
 	* Justin Post, BofA: Asked H1/H2 phasing and World Cup; (CFO James) said the year scales with “the World Cup… mid-June to mid-July” and typical seasonality, and added StubHub is “excited to have an outsized portion of that share.”
 	* Logan Whalley, TD Cowen: Asked ReachPro progress and U.K. price-cap risk; (CEO Baker) said ReachPro is “progressing the way I think we articulated before,” and on the U.K. said, “there was no price cap legislation that was in the King’s Speech.”
 	* Brian Pitz, BMO: Raised concert cancellations and macro trade-down risk; (CFO James) said, “There is no slide,” and added, “Q2 continues to progress really well,” while (CEO Baker) said, “we haven’t seen any impact,” noting, “half of all tickets on StubHub trade at less than $100.”
 	* Andrew Boone, Citizens JMP: Asked whether DOJ/Ticketmaster matters and about AI chatbots; (CEO Baker) said, “Everything… has not hinged on any outcome,” and on AI said, “we want to be wherever consumers are going to be in the future through any channel and every channel.”
 	* Jason Bazinet, Citigroup: Challenged long-term sales & marketing intensity; (CFO James) attributed changes to deliberate ‘24-’25 investment and said 2026 is focused on “expand margins, and that’s exactly what you’re seeing show up in Q1.”

SENTIMENT ANALYSIS

 	* Analysts: Slightly negative to neutral tone, with probing on durability of marketing efficiency, macro sensitivity, cancellations, take rate sustainability, and regulatory/antitrust mechanics; examples included Bazinet’s “what’s going on?” on sales & marketing intensity and Pitz’s concern about “consumer concerns on higher ticket prices.”
 	* Management: Slightly positive in prepared remarks and largely firm in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing leadership-driven leverage and reiteration of guidance; Baker used definitive framing such as “we are reiterating our full year outlook,” while also downplaying uncertainty with “we haven’t seen any impact.”
 	* Versus last quarter, management tone remained consistent on product-led open distribution and disciplined advertising, while analyst scrutiny broadened to macro/cancellation chatter and more detailed expense structure questions.

QUARTER-OVER-QUARTER COMPARISON

 	* The core strategic message carried over: Q4 framed a “shift… to a more product-led strategy” for open distribution, and Q1 reported tangible steps, including the “Distribution Manager” launch and “direct connections with primary ticketing platforms.”
 	* Guidance ranges were unchanged quarter-over-quarter, with Q1 repeating the full-year “$9.9 billion to $10.1 billion” GMS and “$400 million to $420 million” adjusted EBITDA targets previously issued.
 	* Profitability emphasis intensified: Q4 discussed 2025 as an investment period and 2026 margin inflection; Q1 highlighted realized leverage with a 16% adjusted EBITDA margin and marketing efficiency improvement, aligning with prior-quarter claims that scale supports “operating leverage.”
 	* Regulatory discussion shifted from broader “public discussion around ticketing” in Q4 to specific developments in Q1, including Baker’s note that the U.K. King’s Speech included “no price cap legislation” and references to a proposed antitrust settlement featuring “nonexclusive distribution.”

RISKS AND CONCERNS

 	* Regulatory risk remained a recurring topic, with Baker pushing back on price caps: he argued caps “reduces access to events, increases black market prices and fraud,” and said on the U.K. process that discussion is “pushed even further down the road.”
 	* Demand and macro sensitivity was tested by analysts; management response was that it has “not” seen slowdown and that the category is “extremely resilient,” supported by Baker’s comment that “half of all tickets… trade at less than $100.”
 	* Execution risk around newer initiatives was implicitly acknowledged via timing and materiality language: Baker said advertising is still being tested to “get this right,” and AI chatbot integrations were described as “not material things right now.”

FINAL TAKEAWAY

StubHub management characterized Q1 as an early-year confirmation of its 2026 playbook: continued GMS growth alongside expanding margins, reiterated full-year GMS and adjusted EBITDA guidance, and ongoing deleveraging. Baker emphasized that open distribution is being advanced through product-led tools like the AI-driven Distribution Manager and direct primary-platform integrations, while advertising and AI-discovery partnerships remain in testing and “not material” today. James emphasized operating leverage, cash generation, and debt reduction, including a post-quarter $100 million term-loan repayment and no maturities until March 2030.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/stub/earnings/transcripts]

MORE ON STUBHUB HOLDINGS, INC.

title: Alibaba earnings, Trump & Xi meet, PPI data: What to Watch
content:
Market Domination Overtime host Josh Lipton takes a look at the top stories for investors to watch on Wednesday, May 13, including President Trump's meeting with Chinese President Xi, Alibaba (BABA) and Cisco (CSCO) earnings, April's Producer Price Index (PPI) data, and commentary from Federal Reserve officials.

Video Transcript

00:05 Speaker A

Time now for what to watch Wednesday, May 13th.

00:09 Speaker A

Starting off overseas, President Trump is arriving in Beijing on Wednesday for a high-stakes summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping.

00:19 Speaker A

The two leaders are set to talk on all things trade, AI, and the war in Iran. 16 US CEOs are also joining the trip, including Tesla's Elon Musk and Apple's Tim Cook.

00:32 Speaker A

Back in the US, we'll be getting another wave of earnings reports on Wednesday including Alibaba, Birkenstock, and StubHub.

00:39 Speaker A

Cisco is also reporting quarterly earnings after the market's closed, analysts expecting the networking giant to benefit from strong AI infrastructure demand.

00:46 Speaker A

Investors will also be watching whether Cisco boosts its AI order outlook beyond the company's current $5 billion target.

00:57 Speaker A

On the macro side, we're getting fresh inflation data with April's producer price index or PPI, economist forecasting headline PPI to hold steady at 0.5%,

01:08 Speaker A

while core PPI, which strips out food and energy, ticks up on a month-over month basis.

01:13 Speaker A

And taking a look at the Federal Reserve, we'll also be hearing from a couple of Fed presidents throughout the day as investors look for more clues on how policy makers are thinking about the path forward for interest rates.

title: 3 Stocks Under $10 to Buy in May
content:
The allure of low stock prices is obvious, but also potentially dangerous. A lot of stocks with single-digit stock prices are there for a reason. Many of them are also small or iffy outfits that are more likely to continue getting cheaper in the future.

I think I have some ideas that will fare well, and these companies are small -- but not tiny. Archer Aviation(NYSE: ACHR), Snap(NYSE: SNAP), and Stubhub(NYSE: STUB) all have market caps of at least $2 billion. These are the three stocks under $10 that I think are worth buying in May.

Will AI create the world's first trillionaire? Our team just released a report on a little-known company, called an "Indispensable Monopoly," providing the critical technology Nvidia and Intel both need. 

Continue »Image source: Getty Images.

1. Archer Aviation

Like the promise of its speedy short-haul flights, Archer Aviation stock has its ups and downs. The market is just starting to crystallize for electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) aircraft. Archer and rival Joby Aviation(NYSE: JOBY) have captured investor interest ahead of the scaling of their businesses.

There has been some turbulence. Near-term revenue targets have been pared back by Wall Street pros as the industry gets ready to take off, literally and figuratively. The next few years should see a pretty dramatic top-line ramp-up based on current analyst projections.

2026: $14 million 2027: $114 million 2028: $512 million 2029: $1,609 million

Archer's Midnight aircraft has a clear path on the growth runway when it does get rolling. Its high-end air taxi service has attracted airline carriers as partners. It will be the official air taxi provider for the 2028 Olympic Games, even buying a small regional airport near Los Angeles International Airport to make sure it can handle its moment in the spotlight. Even the U.S. Air Force is exploring the potential of Archer's aircraft for military missions.

It will take a few years for Archer to become profitable, and it will remain a high-beta stock. However, with higher annual revenue forecasts than Joby starting in 2028 -- but trading at a third of the enterprise value -- this Archer is primed to hit its target when it's time to let the arrows fly.

2. Snap

It's fair to say that Snapchat's parent company Snap isn't as popular as other visual social hubs like Instagram, TikTok, or YouTube. It's probably still more popular than you think.

Did you know that there are 956 million monthly active users on Snapchat? The platform that made filters or augmented reality lenses cool is also still growing. The audience has grown by 5% over the past year. Tack on a 7% increase in average revenue per user, and Snap's revenue rose 12% in its latest quarter. It posted back-to-back years of double-digit revenue growth before that. Wall Street pros see low double-digit growth through at least the next couple of years.

Story Continues

Reported profitability has been a problem, but the losses are narrowing. Analysts see Snap turning a profit by next year. It's generating healthy and positive free cash flow, more than doubling in its latest quarter. On an adjusted basis, Snap stock is actually cheap. The shares are trading for 10 times forward adjusted earnings and just 8 times next year's target.

3. StubHub

The popular online marketplace for resale tickets to concerts and sporting events has undergone a few ownership changes over the years. It went public in its present form at $23.50 a share just eight months ago. Since it's on the list of stocks below $10, you already know it has not gone well for its IPO investors.

StubHub has shed nearly two-thirds of its value since going public, but it's been rallying in recent weeks. It wound up on the favorable side of two legal rulings. One win was just StubHub settling a lawsuit alleging that it wasn't disclosing customer costs up front. The hub settled with the Federal Trade Commission for a reasonable $10 million. The other courtroom win came in a case for rival Live Nation(NYSE: LYV). The jury in that case found it liable for monopolistic pricing, and now some states want Live Nation separated from its Ticketmaster subsidiary.

There is still a potentially catastrophic legal headwind for StubHub. The United Kingdom and some Canadian provinces have moved to prevent platforms from selling event tickets above face value. This is naturally the lion's share of any third-party marketplace, so StubHub's business model is at risk if this continues to gain traction closer to home.

It's not the only thing holding StubHub back. After four years of heady growth, revenue dipped 1% last year. If inflation and gas prices keep revving higher, there will likely be less money spent on live events. The good news -- if you stomach the risk of court-mandated obsolescence -- is that you can buy StubHub for just six times next year's projected earnings.

Should you buy stock in Archer Aviation right now?

Before you buy stock in Archer Aviation, consider this:

The Motley Fool Stock Advisor analyst team just identified what they believe are the 10 best stocks for investors to buy now… and Archer Aviation wasn’t one of them. The 10 stocks that made the cut could produce monster returns in the coming years.

Consider when Netflix made this list on December 17, 2004... if you invested $1,000 at the time of our recommendation, you’d have $471,827!* Or when Nvidia made this list on April 15, 2005... if you invested $1,000 at the time of our recommendation, you’d have $1,319,291!*

Now, it’s worth noting Stock Advisor’s total average return is 986% — a market-crushing outperformance compared to 207% for the S&P 500. Don't miss the latest top 10 list, available with Stock Advisor, and join an investing community built by individual investors for individual investors.

See the 10 stocks »

*Stock Advisor returns as of May 11, 2026.

Rick Munarriz has no position in any of the stocks mentioned. The Motley Fool recommends Live Nation Entertainment. The Motley Fool has a disclosure policy.

3 Stocks Under $10 to Buy in May was originally published by The Motley Fool

title: Is StubHub Holdings, Inc. (STUB) A Good Stock To Buy Now?
content:
Is STUB a good stock to buy? We came across a bearish thesis on StubHub Holdings, Inc. on Valueinvestorsclub.com by exp_Loss. In this article, we will summarize the bears’ thesis on STUB. StubHub Holdings, Inc.'s share was trading at $7.31 as of April 29th. STUB’s forward P/E was 18.69 according to Yahoo Finance.StubHub Holdings (STUB) On Analysts' Radar Following Earnings

Pixabay/Public Domain

StubHub Holdings, Inc. (STUB) is a secondary ticketing marketplace that has seen its stock decline roughly 64% since its September 2025 IPO, now trading at depressed valuation multiples of ~6.3x 2026E and ~4.0x 2027E EBITDA. Despite this correction, the market may still be overestimating the company’s medium-term earnings potential. Consensus expectations appear too optimistic given structural challenges across industry growth, regulatory scrutiny, competitive dynamics, and marketing efficiency.

Read More: 15 AI Stocks That Are Quietly Making Investors Rich

Read More: Undervalued AI Stock Poised For Massive Gains: 10000% Upside Potential

While the current valuation implies ~$425M–$472M of 2026–2027 EBITDA, a more conservative base case suggests ~$310M and ~$390M respectively, implying further downside of 25–35%. The secondary ticketing market’s growth outlook is moderating following a post-COVID surge, with normalization in consumer demand and increased pricing transparency from “all-in” pricing regulations weighing on conversion and take rates. Regulatory enforcement, including the BOTS Act and FTC actions, alongside strategic responses from primary players like Ticketmaster, could constrain ticket supply available to secondary platforms.

Additionally, StubHub’s reliance on paid search for traffic acquisition raises concerns about diminishing marketing returns, with evidence of declining incremental efficiency and margin compression.

The company’s push into direct issuance introduces further execution risk, as limited exclusivity, lower-quality inventory, and uncertain pricing power may hinder meaningful contribution. Combined with a concentrated shareholder base, impending lock-up expiry, and elevated competitive intensity, StubHub faces multiple headwinds. Overall, the risk-reward remains skewed to the downside as structural and operational challenges persist.

Previously, we covered a bearish thesis on eBay Inc. (EBAY) by Kostadin Ristovski in November 2024, which highlighted the company’s stagnant GMV, limited growth prospects, and valuation concerns despite strong cash generation. EBAY’s stock price has appreciated by 68.76% since our coverage. exp_Loss shares a similar view but emphasizes on regulatory risks, competitive intensity, and weakening unit economics in StubHub.

Story Continues

StubHub Holdings, Inc. is not on our list of the 40 Most Popular Stocks Among Hedge Funds. As per our database, 31 hedge fund portfolios held STUB at the end of the fourth quarter which was 29 in the previous quarter. While we acknowledge the risk and potential of STUB as an investment, our conviction lies in the belief that some AI stocks hold greater promise for delivering higher returns and doing so within a shorter time frame. If you are looking for an AI stock that is more promising than STUB and that has 10,000% upside potential, check out our report about this cheapest AI stock.

Disclosure: None.

title: Live Nation Illegally Monopolized Concerts and Ticketing, Jury Finds
content:
The verdict paves the way for over 30 states to seek measures that would curb the power of Ticketmaster’s parent, including a possible breakup.

Continue Reading

title: Live Nation Court Loss Leaves Breakup of Ticket Seller to Judge
content:
(Bloomberg) -- Live Nation Entertainment Inc.’s loss in a landmark antitrust trial follows years of regulatory scrutiny of its dominance in the live events business and sets the stage for a possible breakup of the largest US concert promoter and ticket seller.

After a six-week trial in Manhattan, which featured testimony from high-profile figures in the music industry and Live Nation Chief Executive Officer Michael Rapino, a federal jury on Wednesday ruled the company illegally monopolized the live events industry and overcharged fans for tickets to music performances.

The verdict marked a major win for a coalition of 33 states and Washington DC that pursued the case despite the US Justice Department’s decision to pull out after the first week of the trial, with a surprise settlement allowing Live Nation to keep its Ticketmaster unit. But jurors concluded the company illegally monopolized ticketing and tied Live Nation venues to its promotion business, leading concert goers to overpay by $1.72 per ticket.

“This is a historic and resounding victory for artists, fans, and the venues that support them,” California Attorney General Rob Bonta said in a statement. “In the face of dwindling antitrust enforcement by the Trump Administration, this verdict shows just how far states can go to protect our residents from big corporations that are using their power to illegally raise prices and rip-off Americans.”

US District Judge Arun Subramanian, who oversaw the trial, will consider the jury’s findings in calculating how much Live Nation must pay for its illegal conduct and how to remedy the monopoly. The states want as much as $700 million and may seek additional penalties for violations of state antitrust laws. Some also have said they want Live Nation to sell Ticketmaster.

New York Attorney General Letitia James, whose office led the case on behalf of the states, praised the verdict. “A jury found what we have long known to be true: Live Nation and Ticketmaster are breaking the law and costing consumers millions of dollars in the process,” James said in a statement.

‘Not Over’

Live Nation said in a statement it will appeal the verdict, and that the maximum amount of damages it should owe would be $450 million. The company would likely challenge any remedy order by the judge, which could prolong the legal fight for years.

“We’re obviously disappointed,” Dan Wall, a lawyer for Live Nation, said after the verdict. “The game is not over by any means.”

Live Nation shares closed down 6.3%, its biggest decline in more than five months. Shares of rival ticket brokers jumped on the news, with StubHub Holding Inc. climbing 3.5% and Vivid Seats Inc. gained 9.3%.

Story Continues

The jury of seven women and two men deliberated for three and a half days before reaching their verdict, which was influenced in part by some of the many internal documents in which Live Nation described its business strategy, according to the foreperson, who asked not to be named.

Among the emails disclosed during the trial were two ticketing directors who bragged about the high fees the company charges fans at its venues and joked that the company is “robbing them blind.”

“The tone, the language that they used in their emails were not very professional,” said the foreperson. “Having the testimony of the CEO, I think it was very interesting to help us understand the whole context.” The foreperson, who had attended a Duran Duran concert at Forest Hills Stadium in Queens and saw Shakira at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey, said the jury also considered the broad consumer impact of high-priced concert tickets.

A second juror, who also asked not to be named, said the panel was aware of the government’s earlier settlement in the case but said it didn’t affect their deliberations. Rather, the jury carefully reviewed the evidence presented to them during the trial, the juror said.

DOJ Settlement

The case was more than a decade in the making. In 2010, the Justice Department and a group of state attorneys general originally allowed Live Nation, whose concert promotion arm helps artists plan and market tours, to buy Ticketmaster, the largest ticketer. Among the conditions was court supervision and a promise by Live Nation not to force customers of its concert promotion services to use Ticketmaster.

Nine years later, the Justice Department said it had found multiple instances where Live Nation allegedly threatened venue operators who sought to switch ticketers. As part of a settlement, the company agreed to additional oversight over its business.

During the Biden administration, federal and state antitrust enforcers teamed up to investigate Live Nation again over allegations it had threatened venues and used its competing lines of business to disadvantage rivals. The Justice Department and a group of 40 states sued in 2024, teeing up the current case.

Live Nation has long maintained its conduct is lawful, but sought to settle the lawsuit before trial. The company employed several well-connected Republican lawyers and lobbyists to advocate for a settlement. The Justice Department signed a deal with the company to resolve its part of the case on March 5, a Thursday, but didn’t inform the court until the following Monday.

That settlement upended the trial as the states scrambled to take over case. While the state and federal enforcers had worked on the investigation since it began in 2023, the Justice Department handled the bulk of the litigation work, including storing the files, paying for the expert witnesses and contributing more than 30 individuals to the trial team.

With the Justice Department’s withdrawal, the states hired the law firm Winston & Strawn and its co-chairman Jeffrey Kessler to lead the rest of the trial. In a separate case, Kessler and his team successfully sued the National Collegiate Athletics Association for antitrust violations on behalf of student-athletes challenging the group’s policies barring payments. That case reached all the way to the Supreme Court. More recently, Kessler and his firm reached a settlement with Nascar in an antitrust suit brought by Michael Jordan’s racing team.

“It’s a great day for antitrust law, a great day for consumers,” Kessler said as he left the lower Manhattan courthouse.

Under the Justice Department’s settlement, Live Nation will no longer be able to require large amphitheaters and arenas to use it as the exclusive ticketing company. It also agreed to terminate and renegotiate exclusive contracts with 13 venues. In addition, Live Nation said it would develop the technology to allow rival ticketers to integrate with its system, which would allow venues to use multiple ticketing services for a single event.

That settlement still requires court approval. A Watergate-era transparency law requires that antitrust settlements go through a public comment and review process. The Justice Department has yet to submit the court filings to begin that proceeding, which will be overseen by Subramanian.

In a statement after the verdict, Omeed Assefi, the Justice Department’s acting assistant attorney general for antitrust, called the verdict “a fantastic outcome for the American people.”

“DOJ and some states settled their case and got instant relief,” he said. “The remaining states received a liability finding and will now move on to the next phase of a remedies trial. Everyone but Live Nation wins with this scenario.”

Yet, already some have raised concerns about the Justice Department’s settlement. In a letter to the court Wednesday, a group of Democratic senators including Elizabeth Warren of Massachusetts urged close scrutiny of the deal amid reporting that the White House was involved in the negotiations. Others in the industry have raised concerns about the provision that would require them to integrate with Ticketmaster’s software to sell tickets for venues.

“This was a massive win for the state AGs and an historic miss for the DOJ,” said Roger Alford, a professor at Notre Dame Law School who was the Trump administration’s No. 2 antitrust official until he was fired last summer. “The DOJ had the talent, the material, and the audience. It just lacked leadership with the courage to step on stage.”

The case is US v. Live Nation Entertainment, 24-cv-03973, US District Court, Southern District of New York (Manhattan).

--With assistance from Christopher Palmeri and Josh Sisco.

(Updates with additional details.)

More stories like this are available on bloomberg.com

©2026 Bloomberg L.P.

title: StubHub will pay $10 million to customers for 'deceptive' pricing. Here's how to see if you're eligible
content:
While much of the event-going public's attention of late has been on the Ticketmaster settlement (1) and subsequent maneuvering, ticket reseller StubHub has agreed to a settlement of its own, which will result in $10 million in refunds to some customers.

The company was accused of deceptive ticket pricing by the Federal Trade Commission. Authorities, in a release (2), said StubHub had violated the FTC Act and the agency's Rule on Unfair or Deceptive Fees by displaying online ticket prices without disclosing the total amount consumers would pay.

Must Read

Thanks to Jeff Bezos, you can now become a landlord for as little as $100 — and no, you don't have to deal with tenants or fix freezers. Here's how Robert Kiyosaki this 1 asset will surge 400% in a year and begs investors not to miss this ‘explosion’ Taxes are going to change for retirees under Trump’s ‘big beautiful bill’ — here are 4 reasons you can’t afford to waste time

StubHub, the FTC said, advertised tickets on its website for three days last May without disclosing up front how much consumers would actually pay.

"The Commission's Fees Rule makes it very clear that the total price of live-event tickets must be disclosed up-front to enable consumers to make fully informed purchasing decisions," Christopher Mufarrige, director of FTC's Bureau of Consumer Protection, wrote in a statement (3).

"Price transparency is essential to a free and competitive marketplace. Today's settlement underscores the Commission's commitment to ensuring that consumers pay the price they are promised."

StubHub, in a statement to Moneywise, said it supported all-in pricing and that the settlement with the FTC covered "a limited number of transactions, spanning just three days in May 2025, where some listings on our site may have displayed ticket prices exclusive of fees."

"While we strongly disagree with the FTC's view of the case, we are addressing their concerns by refunding a portion of those buyers' fee," the statement went on to read.

Who's eligible for a refund?

To qualify for a refund, you'll have to have bought a ticket on StubHub for a live event between the dates of May 12 and May 14, 2025. The ticketing site will notify qualified buyers via email over the next two weeks. They will not have to fill out a claim to receive the payment.

According to an injunction (4) filed in the Southern District of New York, StubHub is also required to launch a website with settlement information, and maintain a customer service line and email for questions.

Story Continues

People whose ticket price did not disclose the full, final price will receive a full refund of the site's mandatory fees. Others who bought a ticket in that time frame will receive a prorated refund of the fees. The exact amounts of the refunds will depend on the number of people who qualify for the two groups.

Refunds will be issued within 90 days.

Read More: How to apply Dave Ramsey’s 7 Baby Steps to your own life

In the shadow of Ticketmaster

StubHub's settlement with the FTC comes just weeks after Ticketmaster reached a $280 million settlement with the U.S. Department of Justice over antitrust laws. Ticketmaster parent company Live Nation, as part of that agreement, said it would cap service fees at owned venues at 15% and allow competitors to sell tickets at its venues (5).

The $280 million is a fine and there has been no talk, so far, of any customer refunds.

While the federal government has settled its case, 34 states are continuing their antitrust trial (6) against Live Nation-Ticketmaster, alleging a monopoly on live events. That case is currently in the hands of the jury (7), which did not reach a verdict in its first day of deliberations on April 10.
